title: The Knight Snags a Queen
setup: Black's queen sits on d5 — exactly one knight-leap from White's e3 horse, with no defender in sight.
hint: From e3, count the knight squares: a single L-jump lands on d5. Is the queen defended?
explanation:
## The Idea

A **loose queen** is the most expensive gift in chess. The knight on e3 can step to d5 in one move (e3 → d5 is the L-shape: file -1, rank +2), and Black's king on e8 is far too distant to defend.

After {{Nxd5}}, White is up a queen for a pawn-shaped sliver of nothing.

## Why Black Has No Defense

- The queen on d5 has no friend nearby — the king on e8 is three squares away.
- The knight on e3 is **defended** by White's king on e1 (although that doesn't matter here, since Black has no piece to recapture with).
- After the capture, White's knight sits proudly on d5 with no follow-up needed.

## Pattern to Memorize

> Always check the **knight's L-radius** when looking at enemy pieces. If a knight one move away can capture an undefended piece, that's the strongest move on the board.
